cards.definition: energy an object has due to its motion — KE = ½mv²
energy stored due to height above ground — GPE = mgh
energy stored in a stretched or compressed object — e.g. a spring or elastic band
energy stored in the internal energy of an object — related to temperature and state
energy cannot be created or destroyed — only transferred from one store to another
the proportion of input energy usefully transferred — Efficiency = useful output ÷ total input × 100%
energy transferred to the surroundings in a less useful form — usually as heat or sound
the rate of energy transfer — Power = Energy ÷ Time, measured in watts (W)
energy needed to raise 1 kg of a substance by 1°C — water has a high SHC
a source of energy for generating electricity — renewable or non-renewable

questions.question: What is the formula for kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½ × mass × velocity². Doubling speed quadruples KE because velocity is squared.

questions.question: What is the conservation of energy?
options: A) Energy increases in every reaction
B) Energy cannot be created or destroyed — only transferred
C) Energy is always lost as heat
D) Only renewable energy is conserved
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy: the total energy in a closed system is constant — it can only be transferred between stores.

questions.question: What is efficiency?
options: C) Useful output energy ÷ total input energy × 100%
questions.explanation: Efficiency measures how well a device converts input energy to useful output. A 100% efficient device wastes no energy.

questions.question: What does power measure?
options: A) The amount of energy stored
B) The rate of energy transfer
C) The efficiency of a device
questions.explanation: Power = Energy ÷ Time (P = E/t). Measured in watts (W). A 1W device transfers 1 joule per second.

questions.question: What is gravitational potential energy?
options: B) Energy stored in a spring
C) Energy due to an object's height above the ground
questions.explanation: GPE = mass × gravitational field strength × height (GPE = mgh). An object gains GPE when lifted.

questions.question: What is specific heat capacity?
options: A) The energy needed to melt 1 kg of a substance
B) The energy needed to raise 1 kg of a substance by 1°C
C) The rate of energy transfer
D) The heat energy released by burning 1 kg of fuel
questions.explanation: Specific heat capacity (SHC) tells us how much energy is needed to heat 1 kg of a material by 1°C. Water's high SHC makes it good for heating systems.

questions.question: What is dissipation of energy?
options: B) Transferring energy to a useful output
C) Energy being wasted to the surroundings — usually as heat or sound
D) Converting energy from one store to another usefully
questions.explanation: Dissipation is the unwanted transfer of energy to the surroundings. Friction and air resistance are common causes.

questions.question: A device has 200 J input and 150 J useful output. What is its efficiency?
questions.explanation: Efficiency = 150 ÷ 200 × 100% = 75%. The remaining 25% is wasted (dissipated).

questions.question: What form does dissipated energy usually take?
questions.explanation: Dissipated energy is mostly transferred to thermal energy stores — heating the surroundings. This makes it harder to use.

questions.question: Which of these is a renewable energy resource?
questions.explanation: Wind is a renewable energy resource — it will not run out. Coal, oil, and natural gas are non-renewable fossil fuels.

questions.question: Kinetic energy depends on mass and velocity.
questions.explanation: KE = ½mv². Both mass and velocity affect kinetic energy — doubling velocity quadruples KE.

questions.question: Energy can be created from nothing if the conditions are right.
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states energy cannot be created or destroyed — only transferred or transformed.

questions.question: A 100% efficient device wastes no energy.
questions.explanation: An efficiency of 100% means all input energy is transferred to the useful output — nothing is wasted.

questions.question: Power is measured in joules.
questions.explanation: Power is measured in watts (W). Joules measure energy. Power = Energy ÷ Time.

questions.question: Dissipated energy is usually transferred usefully.
questions.explanation: Dissipated energy is wasted — usually transferred to thermal stores in the surroundings where it is harder to use.

questions.question: Gravitational potential energy increases when an object is lifted higher.
questions.explanation: GPE = mgh. Increasing height increases GPE. This is stored energy that can be released when the object falls.

questions.question: Specific heat capacity measures how quickly a substance heats up.
questions.explanation: SHC is the energy needed to raise 1 kg of a substance by 1°C — not how quickly it heats. A high SHC means more energy is needed.

questions.question: Efficiency can be greater than 100%.
questions.explanation: Efficiency cannot exceed 100% — this would violate conservation of energy. No device can produce more useful energy than it receives.

questions.question: Wind is a renewable energy resource.
questions.explanation: Wind will not run out — it is continuously replenished. Coal, gas, and oil are non-renewable.

questions.question: Elastic potential energy is stored in a compressed spring.
questions.explanation: Elastic potential energy is stored whenever an elastic object is stretched or compressed — it is released when the object returns to its original shape.

questions.question: ________ energy is energy due to motion — calculated using KE = ½mv².
questions.explanation: Doubling the speed of an object quadruples its kinetic energy because v is squared.

questions.question: ________ energy is the rate of energy transfer, measured in watts.
questions.explanation: Power = Energy ÷ Time. A 60W lightbulb transfers 60 joules of energy per second.

questions.question: ________ = useful output energy ÷ total input energy × 100%.
questions.explanation: Efficiency tells us the percentage of input energy that is usefully transferred.

questions.question: Energy that is wasted — usually as heat — is said to be ________.
questions.explanation: Dissipated energy spreads out to the surroundings and becomes harder to use.

questions.question: The law of conservation of energy states energy cannot be ________ or destroyed.
questions.explanation: Energy is always conserved — it can only be transferred from one store to another.

questions.question: ________ potential energy is stored in objects due to their height above the ground.
questions.explanation: GPE = mgh. An object falling converts GPE to kinetic energy.

questions.question: ________ potential energy is stored in stretched or compressed objects.
questions.explanation: When a spring is compressed or stretched, it stores elastic potential energy.

questions.question: ________ heat capacity is the energy needed to raise 1 kg by 1°C.
questions.explanation: Water has a high specific heat capacity — it absorbs a lot of energy before its temperature rises significantly.

questions.question: ________ energy resources will not run out — e.g. solar, wind, tidal.
questions.explanation: Renewable resources are replenished naturally and do not produce greenhouse gases when generating electricity.

questions.question: ________ energy is energy stored in an object due to its temperature.
questions.explanation: All objects above absolute zero contain thermal energy in the kinetic energy of their particles.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: energy due to motion — KE = ½mv²
energy stored due to height — GPE = mgh
useful output ÷ total input × 100%
rate of energy transfer — measured in watts
energy wasted to surroundings, usually as heat

cards.definition: the rate of flow of electric charge — measured in amperes (A). I = Q/t
energy transferred per unit charge — measured in volts (V). V = W/Q
opposition to the flow of current — measured in ohms (Ω). R = V/I
current through a conductor is proportional to potential difference — V = IR — provided temperature is constant
components connected in a single loop — same current everywhere, voltages add up
components in separate branches — same voltage across each, currents add up
rate at which electrical energy is transferred — P = IV or P = I²R
current that flows in one direction only — e.g. from a battery
current that continuously reverses direction — mains electricity in the UK is 230V, 50 Hz AC
the network of cables and transformers distributing electricity across the UK

questions.question: What is electric current?
options: A) Energy transferred per unit charge
B) The rate of flow of electric charge
D) Energy stored in a battery
questions.explanation: Current (I) is the rate at which electric charge flows. Measured in amperes (A). I = Q/t.

questions.question: What is Ohm's Law?
questions.explanation: Ohm's Law: V = IR. Current is proportional to potential difference at constant temperature for an ohmic conductor.

questions.question: In a series circuit, what is true about current?
options: A) It is different at each component
B) It is the same throughout
C) It is shared between branches
D) It increases after each component
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, components share the same current — there is only one path for current to flow.

questions.question: What is the formula for electrical power?
questions.explanation: Electrical power P = IV (current × voltage). Also P = I²R. Measured in watts (W).

questions.question: What type of current comes from a battery?
questions.explanation: Batteries provide direct current (DC) — current flows in one direction only. Mains electricity is AC.

questions.question: What is potential difference?
options: B) Energy per unit charge transferred
D) Total charge in a circuit
questions.explanation: Potential difference (voltage) = energy transferred per unit charge. Measured in volts (V = J/C).

questions.question: In a parallel circuit, what is true about voltage?
options: B) It is the same across each branch
C) It is zero at each branch
D) It adds up across all branches
questions.explanation: In a parallel circuit, all branches have the same potential difference across them as the supply voltage.

questions.question: What does resistance measure?
options: C) Opposition to the flow of current
questions.explanation: Resistance (R) is the opposition to current flow. Measured in ohms (Ω). R = V/I.

questions.question: What is the UK mains supply voltage and frequency?
questions.explanation: UK mains electricity is 230V AC at 50 Hz. This is alternating current that reverses direction 50 times per second.

questions.question: What is the National Grid?
options: B) A network distributing electricity across the UK
questions.explanation: The National Grid is the system of cables and transformers that distributes electricity from power stations to homes and businesses across the UK.

questions.question: Current is measured in volts.
questions.explanation: Current is measured in amperes (A). Volts measure potential difference (voltage).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current is the same throughout.
questions.explanation: There is only one path in a series circuit — all components carry the same current.

questions.question: Resistance is measured in ohms.
questions.explanation: Resistance (Ω) measures the opposition to current flow. R = V/I.

questions.question: Ohm's Law states V = IR.
questions.explanation: For ohmic conductors at constant temperature, V = IR — voltage is proportional to current.

questions.question: DC current reverses direction continuously.
questions.explanation: DC (direct current) flows in one direction only. AC (alternating current) reverses direction.

questions.question: In a parallel circuit, voltage is the same across each branch.
questions.explanation: Each branch of a parallel circuit has the same potential difference as the supply.

questions.question: Electrical power is calculated as P = IV.
questions.explanation: Power (W) = Current (A) × Voltage (V). Also P = I²R.

questions.question: UK mains electricity is direct current (DC).
questions.explanation: UK mains electricity is alternating current (AC) at 230V and 50 Hz.

questions.question: Adding more resistors in series decreases total resistance.
questions.explanation: Adding resistors in series increases total resistance — they all oppose the current in the same path.

questions.question: The National Grid uses high voltage to reduce energy losses.
questions.explanation: High voltage means lower current for the same power — lower current means less energy wasted as heat in cables.

questions.question: Electric ________ is the rate of flow of charge — measured in amperes.
questions.explanation: Current I = Q/t. More current means more charge flowing per second.

questions.question: ________ difference is energy transferred per unit charge — measured in volts.
questions.explanation: Potential difference (voltage) = energy ÷ charge. V = W/Q.

questions.question: ________ is opposition to current flow — measured in ohms.
questions.explanation: Resistance R = V/I. Higher resistance means less current for a given voltage.

questions.question: Ohm's Law states: V = ________.
questions.explanation: This is true for ohmic conductors at constant temperature: voltage equals current times resistance.

questions.question: In a ________ circuit, the same current flows through all components.
questions.explanation: Series circuits have one path — all components share the same current but the voltages add up.

questions.question: In a ________ circuit, the same voltage is across each branch.
questions.explanation: Parallel circuits have multiple paths — each branch has the same voltage but the currents add up.

questions.question: Electrical power P = ________.
questions.explanation: Power (watts) = current (amps) × voltage (volts). Also P = I²R.

questions.question: ________ current (DC) flows in one direction only.
questions.explanation: Batteries produce DC. Mains electricity is AC — it reverses direction 50 times per second in the UK.

questions.question: UK mains electricity is ________ V alternating current at 50 Hz.
questions.explanation: All UK mains sockets supply 230V AC. This is stepped up and down by transformers in the National Grid.

questions.question: The National Grid uses high ________ to reduce energy loss in transmission cables.
questions.explanation: High voltage = low current for same power. Less current = less heat wasted in cables (P = I²R).

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: rate of flow of electric charge — measured in amps
energy per unit charge — measured in volts
opposition to current — measured in ohms
same current throughout — voltages add up
same voltage across branches — currents add up

cards.definition: a model describing matter as made of tiny particles — explains the properties and behaviour of solids, liquids, and gases
a state of matter — particles closely packed in a regular arrangement, vibrating in fixed positions
a state of matter — particles close together but able to flow past each other
a state of matter — particles far apart, moving rapidly and randomly, filling their container
the temperature at which a solid changes to a liquid — particles have enough energy to overcome the forces holding them
the temperature at which a liquid changes to a gas throughout its volume
mass per unit volume — Density = mass ÷ volume, measured in kg/m³
the total kinetic and potential energy of all the particles in a substance
energy needed to change the state of 1 kg of a substance without changing temperature
caused by gas particles colliding with the walls of their container — increases with temperature or decreased volume

questions.question: In a solid, how are particles arranged?
options: A) Far apart and moving randomly
B) Close together and flowing freely
C) Closely packed in a regular arrangement, vibrating
D) In random positions but not moving
questions.explanation: In solids, particles are in fixed positions in a regular lattice, vibrating about their positions. They cannot flow.

questions.question: What happens to particles when a substance melts?
options: A) They gain enough energy to escape the substance
B) They gain enough energy to overcome the forces holding them in fixed positions
C) They slow down and stop moving
questions.explanation: At the melting point, particles gain enough energy to overcome the intermolecular forces keeping them in fixed positions and begin to flow.

questions.question: What causes gas pressure?
options: B) Collisions of gas particles with container walls
D) Particles sticking to the container walls
questions.explanation: Gas pressure is caused by the constant collisions of gas particles with the walls of their container. More collisions = higher pressure.

questions.question: What is density?
questions.explanation: Density = mass ÷ volume (ρ = m/V), measured in kg/m³. Denser materials have more mass per unit volume.

questions.question: What is specific latent heat?
options: A) Energy to raise temperature by 1°C
B) Energy to change state of 1 kg without temperature change
C) Heat lost to the surroundings
D) The boiling point of a substance
questions.explanation: Specific latent heat is the energy needed to change the state of 1 kg without a temperature change — temperature is constant during a state change.

questions.question: What is internal energy?
options: A) Energy stored in the nucleus
B) Total kinetic and potential energy of all particles
C) Energy transferred by heating only
D) Electrical energy stored in a substance
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the sum of all kinetic energy (movement) and potential energy (position) of particles in a substance.

questions.question: What happens to gas pressure if temperature increases (volume constant)?
options: D) Pressure first increases then decreases
questions.explanation: Higher temperature → particles move faster → more frequent and more energetic collisions with walls → pressure increases.

questions.question: In which state do particles have the most energy?
options: D) All states have equal energy
questions.explanation: Gas particles have the most energy — they move rapidly and randomly. Solid particles have the least energy.

questions.question: What is the formula for density?
questions.explanation: Density (ρ) = mass (m) ÷ volume (V). Measured in kg/m³ or g/cm³.

questions.question: During melting, what happens to temperature?
questions.explanation: During a state change (e.g. melting), temperature remains constant. The energy being supplied is used to change state, not increase temperature.

questions.question: Solid particles vibrate in fixed positions.
questions.explanation: Solid particles are closely packed in a regular lattice and vibrate about fixed positions — they cannot flow.

questions.question: Gas particles are closely packed together.
questions.explanation: Gas particles are widely spaced and move rapidly and randomly. They are much further apart than in solids or liquids.

questions.question: Density = mass × volume.
questions.explanation: Density = mass ÷ volume (ρ = m/V). Greater density means more mass packed into the same volume.

questions.question: Gas pressure increases when temperature increases at constant volume.
questions.explanation: Higher temperature → faster particles → more frequent, energetic collisions with walls → higher pressure.

questions.question: Temperature changes during a change of state.
questions.explanation: Temperature remains constant during a change of state. Energy is used to break/form intermolecular forces, not increase kinetic energy.

questions.question: Liquid particles can flow past each other.
questions.explanation: In liquids, particles are close together but have enough energy to move past each other — this gives liquids their fluid properties.

questions.question: Specific latent heat is the energy to raise 1 kg by 1°C.
questions.explanation: Specific latent heat is the energy to change the state of 1 kg at constant temperature. Specific heat capacity is energy to raise temperature.

questions.question: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of all particles.
questions.explanation: Internal energy = sum of kinetic energy (movement) + potential energy (position) of all particles.

questions.question: Particles in a gas move slowly and randomly.
questions.explanation: Gas particles move rapidly and randomly — they have the most kinetic energy of all three states.

questions.question: Pressure in a gas is caused by particle collisions with container walls.
questions.explanation: Every collision of a gas particle with the container wall exerts a force — the total of all these forces creates pressure.

questions.question: In a ________, particles are closely packed in a regular arrangement and vibrate in fixed positions.
questions.explanation: Solid particles cannot flow — they are held in place by strong intermolecular forces.

questions.question: In a ________, particles are close but can flow past each other.
questions.explanation: Liquids have a fixed volume but no fixed shape — they take the shape of their container.

questions.question: In a ________, particles are far apart and move rapidly in random directions.
questions.explanation: Gases expand to fill their container and exert pressure through collisions with the walls.

questions.question: ________ = mass ÷ volume.
questions.explanation: Density (ρ = m/V) is measured in kg/m³. Materials with high density have lots of mass in a small volume.

questions.question: Gas ________ is caused by particles colliding with the container walls.
questions.explanation: More collisions (higher temperature or smaller volume) means higher pressure.

questions.question: ________ latent heat is the energy needed to change the state of 1 kg without a temperature change.
questions.explanation: During a state change, energy breaks or forms intermolecular bonds — temperature stays constant.

questions.question: ________ energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of all the particles in a substance.
questions.explanation: Adding energy to a substance increases its internal energy — either by raising temperature or changing state.

questions.question: At the ________ point, a solid changes to a liquid.
questions.explanation: The melting point is the temperature at which particles have enough energy to overcome the forces keeping them in fixed positions.

questions.question: When temperature increases at constant volume, gas ________ increases.
questions.explanation: Higher temperature → faster particles → more frequent and more forceful collisions with walls → higher pressure.

questions.question: During a change of state, ________ remains constant even though energy is being supplied.
questions.explanation: The energy supplied during a state change overcomes intermolecular forces — it doesn't increase kinetic energy or temperature.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: particles in regular fixed positions — vibrate only
particles close but able to flow
particles far apart, moving rapidly and randomly
mass ÷ volume
energy to change state of 1 kg at constant temperature

cards.definition: an object remains at rest or moves at constant velocity unless a resultant force acts on it
resultant force = mass × acceleration — F = ma
every action has an equal and opposite reaction — forces act in pairs on different objects
the single force that represents the combined effect of all forces acting on an object
the product of mass and velocity — p = mv. A vector quantity
in a closed system, total momentum before a collision equals total momentum after
total distance to stop = thinking distance + braking distance
the force of gravity on an object — W = mg
the constant speed reached when driving force equals resistive force — no acceleration
energy transferred by a force — W = Fd (force × distance in direction of force)

questions.question: What does Newton's First Law state?
options: B) Force and acceleration are proportional
C) Objects remain at rest or constant velocity unless a resultant force acts
D) Every action has an equal and opposite reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's First Law: an object continues at rest or constant velocity unless acted on by a resultant (unbalanced) force.

questions.question: What is the formula from Newton's Second Law?
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma. Force equals mass times acceleration. More force → more acceleration for same mass.

questions.question: What is terminal velocity?
options: A) Maximum possible speed in a vacuum
B) Speed when driving force equals resistive force — constant speed
C) Speed at the moment of impact
D) Minimum speed needed to stay in motion
questions.explanation: Terminal velocity is reached when the driving force equals resistive forces (drag). Resultant force is zero — no more acceleration.

questions.question: What is momentum?
questions.explanation: Momentum p = mv (mass × velocity). Momentum is a vector — it has both magnitude and direction.

questions.question: What is the stopping distance of a car?
options: A) Distance from braking to stop
C) Distance covered in the driver's reaction time
questions.explanation: Stopping distance = thinking distance (driver reacts) + braking distance (car decelerates). Both increase with speed.

questions.question: What is weight?
options: A) The amount of matter in an object
B) The gravitational force on an object — W = mg
C) The size of the normal contact force
questions.explanation: Weight = mass × gravitational field strength (W = mg). Weight is a force measured in newtons.

questions.question: What does Newton's Third Law state?
options: B) Objects at rest stay at rest
C) Every action has an equal and opposite reaction
D) Momentum is conserved in collisions
questions.explanation: Newton's Third Law: for every force (action) there is an equal and opposite force (reaction) on a different object.

questions.question: What is work done?
questions.explanation: Work done = force × distance moved in the direction of the force (W = Fd). Measured in joules.

questions.question: What is conservation of momentum?
options: A) Momentum always increases in collisions
B) Total momentum before equals total momentum after in a closed system
C) Momentum is zero after every collision
D) Only elastic collisions conserve momentum
questions.explanation: Conservation of momentum: in a closed system (no external forces), total momentum before = total momentum after a collision.

questions.question: What is the resultant force?
options: A) The largest force acting on an object
B) The single force representing all forces combined
C) The force of friction only
questions.explanation: The resultant force is the vector sum of all forces. If resultant = 0, there is no acceleration (Newton's First Law).

questions.question: Newton's Second Law is F = ma.
questions.explanation: F = ma: resultant force = mass × acceleration. This means greater force → greater acceleration for the same mass.

questions.question: Terminal velocity occurs when acceleration is at its maximum.
questions.explanation: Terminal velocity occurs when acceleration is zero — driving force equals resistive force, so resultant force = 0.

questions.question: Stopping distance = thinking distance + braking distance.
questions.explanation: Thinking distance is the distance covered during the driver's reaction time. Braking distance is covered while decelerating.

questions.question: Weight is the same as mass.
questions.explanation: Mass is the amount of matter (measured in kg). Weight is the gravitational force (measured in N). W = mg.

questions.question: Momentum is a scalar quantity.
questions.explanation: Momentum (p = mv) is a vector — it has both magnitude and direction. Velocity is also a vector.

questions.question: Newton's Third Law means forces always cancel out.
questions.explanation: Newton's Third Law pairs act on different objects — they don't cancel. Cancellation only applies to forces on the same object.

questions.question: Work done = force × distance.
questions.explanation: Work done (J) = force (N) × distance moved in the direction of the force (m). W = Fd.

questions.question: Conservation of momentum applies in closed systems.
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, total momentum before = total momentum after any collision.

questions.question: A resultant force of zero means an object is not moving.
questions.explanation: Zero resultant force means constant velocity — the object could be stationary OR moving at constant speed.

questions.question: Increasing speed increases both thinking and braking distance.
questions.explanation: Higher speed → greater thinking distance (same reaction time, more distance covered) and greater braking distance.

questions.question: Newton's Second Law: F = ________.
questions.explanation: Resultant force = mass × acceleration. Greater force causes greater acceleration for the same mass.

questions.question: ________ velocity is reached when driving force equals resistive force — no further acceleration.
questions.explanation: At terminal velocity, resultant force = 0 and the object moves at constant speed.

questions.question: Momentum p = ________.
questions.explanation: Momentum (kg m/s) = mass (kg) × velocity (m/s). It is a vector quantity.

questions.question: Stopping distance = thinking distance + ________ distance.
questions.explanation: Both increase with speed. Braking distance also increases with poor road conditions or worn tyres.

questions.question: Weight W = ________.
questions.explanation: Weight (N) = mass (kg) × gravitational field strength (g = 9.8 N/kg on Earth).

questions.question: Newton's Third Law: every action has an equal and ________ reaction.
questions.explanation: These force pairs act on different objects — e.g. rocket pushes gas backward, gas pushes rocket forward.

questions.question: Work done W = ________.
questions.explanation: Work done (J) = force (N) × distance (m) in the direction of the force.

questions.question: The ________ force is the single force representing all forces acting on an object.
questions.explanation: If resultant force = 0, the object is in equilibrium (at rest or constant velocity).

questions.question: In a closed system, total ________ before a collision equals total momentum after.
questions.explanation: Conservation of momentum: Σp before = Σp after, as long as no external forces act.

questions.question: Newton's First Law: an object stays at rest or constant velocity unless a ________ force acts.
questions.explanation: A resultant force is needed to change the speed or direction of an object.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: no resultant force → constant velocity or rest
F = ma — resultant force = mass × acceleration
every action has equal and opposite reaction on different object
p = mv — vector quantity, conserved in closed systems
driving force equals resistive force — constant speed

cards.definition: a wave where oscillation is perpendicular to the direction of energy transfer — e.g. light, water waves
a wave where oscillation is parallel to the direction of energy transfer — e.g. sound
the distance between two successive wave crests (or compressions) — measured in metres
the number of complete waves passing a point per second — measured in hertz (Hz)
the maximum displacement from the rest position — related to the energy carried by the wave
the speed at which a wave travels — Wave speed = frequency × wavelength (v = fλ)
the family of transverse waves from radio (longest wavelength) to gamma rays (shortest)
the change in direction of a wave as it passes from one medium to another due to a change in speed
when a wave hits a boundary at greater than the critical angle and is fully reflected back
radiation with enough energy to remove electrons from atoms — gamma, X-rays, and UV. Can cause cancer

questions.question: What is a transverse wave?
options: A) Wave where oscillation is parallel to energy transfer
B) Wave where oscillation is perpendicular to energy transfer
C) A wave that cannot travel through a vacuum
D) A wave that only travels through solids
questions.explanation: In transverse waves, particles oscillate at right angles to the direction of energy transfer. Examples: light, water, seismic S-waves.

questions.question: What is the formula for wave speed?
questions.explanation: Wave speed v = frequency × wavelength (v = fλ). All electromagnetic waves travel at 3 × 10⁸ m/s in a vacuum.

questions.question: What is amplitude?
options: C) Maximum displacement from rest position
D) Number of waves per second
questions.explanation: Amplitude is the maximum displacement from the equilibrium position. Greater amplitude means more energy carried by the wave.

questions.question: Which wave type does sound belong to?
questions.explanation: Sound is a longitudinal wave — particles oscillate parallel to the direction of energy transfer through compressions and rarefactions.

questions.question: What is refraction?
options: A) Reflection of a wave at a boundary
B) Change in direction due to change in speed at a boundary
C) Absorption of a wave by a medium
D) Diffraction of a wave through a gap
questions.explanation: Refraction occurs when a wave changes speed as it crosses a boundary between two materials — it bends towards the normal when slowing down.

questions.question: What is total internal reflection?
options: A) All light being absorbed by a material
B) Reflection occurring inside a transparent object above the critical angle
C) Light bending as it enters glass
D) Light being split into colours
questions.explanation: TIR occurs when light hits the boundary at an angle greater than the critical angle — all light is reflected back. Used in optical fibres.

questions.question: Which part of the EM spectrum has the highest frequency?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays have the highest frequency (and shortest wavelength) of all EM waves. They are the most energetic and most ionising.

questions.question: What is ionising radiation?
options: B) Radiation with enough energy to remove electrons from atoms
C) Radiation that only affects gases
D) Radiation used in radio transmission
questions.explanation: Ionising radiation (gamma, X-rays, UV) has enough energy to knock electrons from atoms, forming ions. This can damage DNA and cause cancer.

questions.question: What is frequency measured in?
questions.explanation: Frequency is measured in hertz (Hz) — 1 Hz means one complete wave per second. Higher frequency means more waves per second.

questions.question: All EM waves travel at the same speed in what?
questions.explanation: All electromagnetic waves travel at the speed of light (3 × 10⁸ m/s) in a vacuum. They slow down in other media.

questions.question: In a longitudinal wave, particles oscillate parallel to the direction of energy transfer.
questions.explanation: Sound is a longitudinal wave — compressions and rarefactions form parallel to the direction the sound travels.

questions.question: Amplitude is the distance between two wave crests.
questions.explanation: Amplitude is the maximum displacement from the rest position. The distance between crests is wavelength.

questions.question: Wave speed = frequency × wavelength.
questions.explanation: v = fλ. All EM waves travel at 3 × 10⁸ m/s in a vacuum, so higher frequency means shorter wavelength.

questions.question: Radio waves have the highest frequency in the EM spectrum.
questions.explanation: Gamma rays have the highest frequency. Radio waves have the lowest frequency and longest wavelength.

questions.question: Refraction is caused by a change in wave speed at a boundary.
questions.explanation: When a wave moves from one medium to another, its speed changes, causing it to change direction (refraction).

questions.question: Total internal reflection requires the angle to be less than the critical angle.
questions.explanation: TIR occurs when the angle of incidence is greater than the critical angle — the wave is completely reflected.

questions.question: Ionising radiation can cause cancer by damaging DNA.
questions.explanation: Ionising radiation removes electrons from atoms, which can damage DNA in cells, potentially causing mutations and cancer.

questions.question: Sound waves are transverse waves.
questions.explanation: Sound waves are longitudinal — particles vibrate parallel to the direction the wave travels.

questions.question: All electromagnetic waves travel at the same speed in a vacuum.
questions.explanation: All EM waves travel at 3 × 10⁸ m/s (the speed of light) in a vacuum, regardless of frequency.

questions.question: Higher amplitude means more energy carried by the wave.
questions.explanation: Amplitude is related to the energy of a wave — a bigger amplitude means more energy is transferred.

questions.question: A ________ wave has oscillations perpendicular to the direction of energy transfer.
questions.explanation: Examples include light, water waves, and seismic S-waves.

questions.question: A ________ wave has oscillations parallel to the direction of energy transfer.
questions.explanation: Sound is the most common example of a longitudinal wave.

questions.question: ________ is the distance between two successive wave crests.
questions.explanation: Wavelength (λ) is measured in metres. Longer wavelength means lower frequency.

questions.question: Wave speed v = f × ________.
questions.explanation: Wave speed = frequency × wavelength. For EM waves in vacuum, v = 3 × 10⁸ m/s.

questions.question: ________ is the maximum displacement of particles from their rest position.
questions.explanation: Greater amplitude means more energy carried by the wave.

questions.question: ________ occurs when a wave changes speed at a boundary, causing it to change direction.
questions.explanation: Waves slow down and bend towards the normal when entering a denser medium.

questions.question: ________ radiation has enough energy to remove electrons from atoms — e.g. gamma rays.
questions.explanation: Ionising radiation can damage DNA and cause cancer.

questions.question: ________ internal reflection occurs when light hits a boundary above the critical angle.
questions.explanation: TIR is the basis of optical fibres — light is trapped inside by repeated reflections.

questions.question: Frequency is measured in ________.
questions.explanation: Hz (hertz) means cycles per second. 1 kHz = 1000 Hz.

questions.question: The ________ spectrum ranges from radio waves to gamma rays.
questions.explanation: All EM waves are transverse and travel at 3 × 10⁸ m/s in a vacuum.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: oscillation perpendicular to energy transfer — e.g. light
oscillation parallel to energy transfer — e.g. sound
v = fλ — frequency × wavelength
wave changes direction due to speed change at boundary
all reflection when angle exceeds critical angle

cards.definition: the region around a magnet or current-carrying wire where magnetic forces act
magnetism produced by an electric current — the basis of motors, generators, and transformers
a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force — used in electric motors
used to find the direction of force on a current-carrying conductor: thumb=force, index=field, middle=current
generating a voltage by changing the magnetic flux through a conductor — basis of generators and transformers
a device that changes AC voltage using electromagnetic induction — step-up or step-down
increases voltage and decreases current — used to transmit electricity at high voltage
decreases voltage and increases current — used to deliver safe voltages to homes
the scientific theory that the universe began from an extremely hot, dense point ~13.8 billion years ago
the increase in wavelength of light from distant galaxies — evidence that the universe is expanding

questions.question: What is the motor effect?
options: A) Generating voltage by moving a magnet
B) A current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiencing a force
C) Electricity flowing through a transformer
D) Magnetic field lines repelling each other
questions.explanation: The motor effect: a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force. This is the basis of electric motors.

questions.question: What does electromagnetic induction produce?
questions.explanation: Electromagnetic induction generates a voltage (and current if circuit is complete) when a conductor moves in a magnetic field or the field changes.

questions.question: What is a transformer used for?
options: B) Changing AC voltage using electromagnetic induction
questions.explanation: Transformers use electromagnetic induction to change the voltage of an alternating current supply. They only work with AC.

questions.question: What is red-shift evidence for?
options: D) Galaxies are moving towards us
questions.explanation: Red-shift shows that galaxies are moving away from us. The further away a galaxy, the greater its red-shift — evidence the universe is expanding.

questions.question: What is the Big Bang theory?
options: A) The universe has always existed
B) The universe began in a single explosion from a dense point
C) Stars were formed before the universe
D) All matter was created simultaneously
questions.explanation: The Big Bang theory: the universe began from an extremely hot, dense point ~13.8 billion years ago and has been expanding since.

questions.question: What does Fleming's left-hand rule predict?
options: A) Direction of current in a transformer
B) Direction of force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field
C) Strength of a magnetic field
D) Voltage output of a generator
questions.explanation: Fleming's left-hand rule: point fingers so index = field, middle finger = current — the thumb points in the direction of the force (motion).

questions.question: What does a step-up transformer do?
options: A) Increases current and decreases voltage
C) Increases voltage and decreases current
D) Reduces energy losses in wires
questions.explanation: A step-up transformer increases voltage and decreases current. The National Grid uses step-up transformers to reduce energy losses in transmission cables.

questions.question: What evidence supports the Big Bang theory?
options: B) Red-shift and cosmic microwave background radiation
C) The Sun being at the centre of the universe
D) Galaxies spinning faster over time
questions.explanation: Two key pieces of evidence for the Big Bang: red-shift of galaxies (universe expanding) and cosmic microwave background radiation (CMBR) — the afterglow of the Big Bang.

questions.question: What is electromagnetic induction?
options: A) The force on a wire in a magnetic field
B) Generating a voltage by changing magnetic flux through a conductor
C) The flow of current through a coil
D) Resistance of a wire in a magnetic field
questions.explanation: Electromagnetic induction: moving a conductor relative to a magnetic field generates a voltage. This is the basis of generators and transformers.

questions.question: What is cosmic microwave background radiation (CMBR)?
options: B) Electromagnetic radiation from all stars
C) Low-level radiation throughout the universe — evidence for the Big Bang
D) Radiation emitted by galaxies in the infrared
questions.explanation: CMBR is the residual heat radiation from the Big Bang. It is detected uniformly from all directions in space — strong evidence for the Big Bang theory.

questions.question: The motor effect causes a force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field.
questions.explanation: The motor effect is used in electric motors — a current in a magnetic field experiences a force.

questions.question: Electromagnetic induction generates a current when a conductor is in a static magnetic field.
questions.explanation: Induction requires a changing magnetic flux — either the conductor must move or the field must change.

questions.question: Transformers work with direct current (DC).
questions.explanation: Transformers only work with AC — the continuously changing current creates a continuously changing magnetic field needed for induction.

questions.question: Red-shift provides evidence that the universe is expanding.
questions.explanation: The red-shift of distant galaxies shows they are moving away — the greater the red-shift, the faster they recede.

questions.question: The Big Bang theory states the universe has always existed.
questions.explanation: The Big Bang theory states the universe began from an extremely hot, dense point approximately 13.8 billion years ago.

questions.question: A step-up transformer increases voltage and decreases current.
questions.explanation: Step-up transformers increase voltage for efficient long-distance transmission. Lower current means less energy wasted as heat.

questions.question: Fleming's left-hand rule applies to generators.
questions.explanation: Fleming's left-hand rule applies to motors (motor effect). Fleming's right-hand rule applies to generators.

questions.question: CMBR is evidence for the Big Bang theory.
questions.explanation: Cosmic microwave background radiation is the residual heat from the Big Bang, detected uniformly from all directions.

questions.question: The National Grid uses step-down transformers to transmit electricity.
questions.explanation: The National Grid uses step-up transformers to increase voltage for transmission (reducing current and heat losses). Step-down transformers reduce voltage for safe home use.

questions.question: Red-shift means the wavelength of light from a galaxy is shorter than expected.
questions.explanation: Red-shift means the wavelength is longer (shifted towards red). A galaxy moving away stretches its light waves.

questions.question: The ________ effect is the force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field.
questions.explanation: The motor effect is the basis of all electric motors.

questions.question: ________ induction generates a voltage when a conductor experiences a changing magnetic field.
questions.explanation: This is the basis of generators and transformers.

questions.question: A ________ uses electromagnetic induction to change AC voltage.
questions.explanation: Transformers only work with AC — they need a changing current to create a changing magnetic field.

questions.question: A step-________ transformer increases voltage for efficient electricity transmission.
questions.explanation: Higher voltage means lower current for same power — less energy wasted as heat in cables.

questions.question: ________ shift is the increase in wavelength of light from distant galaxies — evidence of expansion.
questions.explanation: The greater the red-shift, the faster the galaxy is moving away — and the further it is.

questions.question: The ________ Bang theory states the universe began from an extremely hot, dense point.
questions.explanation: The Big Bang occurred approximately 13.8 billion years ago.

questions.question: ________ microwave background radiation is residual heat from the Big Bang.
questions.explanation: CMBR is detected uniformly from all directions — one of the strongest pieces of evidence for the Big Bang.

questions.question: Fleming's ________ hand rule finds the direction of force on a motor conductor.
questions.explanation: Left hand: index = field, middle = current, thumb = force/motion.

questions.question: A step-________ transformer reduces voltage for safe delivery to homes.
questions.explanation: Step-down transformers at local substations reduce the high National Grid voltage to 230V for homes.

questions.question: ________ is produced around a current-carrying conductor.
questions.explanation: The magnetic field around a wire can be increased by forming it into a coil (solenoid).

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: force on current-carrying conductor in magnetic field
voltage generated by changing magnetic flux
increases voltage, decreases current
wavelength increase — evidence universe is expanding
universe began from hot, dense point ~13.8 billion years ago

blocks.block_name: Separate Science: Moments, Pressure & Nuclear Physics

cards.definition: turning effect of force — moment = force × perpendicular distance from pivot (N⋅m)
for balance: sum of clockwise moments = sum of anticlockwise moments
pressure transmitted equally through a liquid — used to magnify force
P = F/A. Liquid pressure: P = ρgh
splitting a heavy nucleus — releases large energy — used in nuclear power stations
joining light nuclei — releases more energy — occurs in stars — not yet achieved for power
time for half the radioactive atoms to decay — constant for each isotope
low-level radiation from natural sources — cosmic rays, radon gas, rocks, food
exposure to radiation without becoming radioactive — e.g. food irradiation
radioactive material deposited on or inside a person — source of ongoing exposure

questions.question: What is a moment?
options: B) Turning effect of force — force × perpendicular distance from pivot
questions.explanation: Moment (N⋅m) = force × perpendicular distance from pivot. Longer lever = greater moment.

questions.question: What is the principle of moments?
options: B) Clockwise moments = anticlockwise for balance
C) Centre of mass at pivot
questions.explanation: For rotational equilibrium: Σ clockwise = Σ anticlockwise moments.

questions.question: How do hydraulic systems work?
options: A) Compressing gas in a cylinder
B) Pressure transmitted equally through a liquid — small force creates large force
C) Using pulleys to multiply force
D) Converting electrical to mechanical energy
questions.explanation: Pressure = F/A. Liquid transmits pressure equally — small piston (small area) → large piston (large area) → larger force.

questions.question: What is nuclear fission?
options: C) Splitting a heavy nucleus — releases large energy — used in power stations
questions.explanation: U-235 absorbs a neutron → splits → smaller nuclei + neutrons + energy. Chain reaction controlled in reactors.

questions.question: What is nuclear fusion?
options: C) Joining light nuclei — releases very large energy — powers stars
questions.explanation: Fusion requires extreme conditions — not yet achieved sustainably for power generation on Earth.

questions.question: What is half-life?
options: A) Time for all atoms to decay
B) Time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay — constant for each isotope
questions.explanation: Half-life is constant — used for carbon dating, medical imaging, nuclear waste management.

questions.question: What is background radiation?
options: A) Radiation only from nuclear power stations
B) Low-level radiation from natural sources — cosmic rays, radon, rocks, food
C) Radiation from X-ray machines only
questions.explanation: Background radiation is always present — mostly natural (radon gas from rocks is the largest source in the UK).

questions.question: What is the difference between irradiation and contamination?
options: B) Irradiation: exposure to radiation without becoming radioactive; contamination: radioactive material on/in person
C) Irradiation is more dangerous always
questions.explanation: Irradiation stops when source removed. Contamination continues — the source is with the person.

questions.question: What is liquid pressure?
options: B) P = ρgh — increases with depth and density
questions.explanation: Liquid pressure depends on density (ρ), gravitational field strength (g), and depth (h).

questions.question: Why is nuclear fusion not used for power generation yet?
options: A) It produces too much waste
B) Fusion requires extremely high temperatures and pressures — difficult to sustain
C) It releases too little energy
questions.explanation: Fusion requires temperatures > 100 million °C — containing the reaction is the main engineering challenge.

questions.question: Moment = force × perpendicular distance from pivot.
questions.explanation: Units: N⋅m.

questions.question: For balance, clockwise moments exceed anticlockwise.
questions.explanation: For balance: clockwise = anticlockwise.

questions.question: Hydraulic systems compress liquids to multiply force.
questions.explanation: Liquids are incompressible — pressure is TRANSMITTED equally.

questions.question: Nuclear fission releases energy by joining nuclei.
questions.explanation: FISSION splits nuclei. FUSION joins nuclei.

questions.question: Half-life is affected by temperature and pressure.
questions.explanation: Half-life is constant — independent of physical conditions.

questions.question: Background radiation is only from man-made sources.
questions.explanation: Most background radiation is NATURAL — radon, cosmic rays, rocks.

questions.question: Irradiation makes an object radioactive.
questions.explanation: Irradiation is exposure to radiation — the object does NOT become radioactive.

questions.question: Nuclear fusion powers the Sun.
questions.explanation: The Sun fuses hydrogen to helium — releasing vast energy.

questions.question: Liquid pressure increases with depth.
questions.explanation: P = ρgh — more depth = more liquid above = more pressure.

questions.question: Contamination is more dangerous than irradiation because the source stays with the person.
questions.explanation: Contamination: radioactive material on/in body — ongoing exposure.

questions.question: ________ = force × perpendicular distance from pivot — turning effect.
questions.explanation: N⋅m.

questions.question: Principle of moments: ________ moments = anticlockwise for balance.
questions.explanation: Σ clockwise = Σ anticlockwise.

questions.question: Hydraulic systems: pressure ________ equally through liquid — multiplies force.
questions.explanation: Liquids are incompressible — pressure acts in all directions.

questions.question: Liquid pressure P = ρg________ — increases with depth.
questions.explanation: ρ = density, g = gravitational field strength.

questions.question: Nuclear ________ splits heavy nuclei — used in power stations.
questions.explanation: Controlled chain reaction — U-235.

questions.question: Nuclear ________ joins light nuclei — powers the Sun.
questions.explanation: Requires extreme temperature/pressure.

questions.question: ________ is the time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay.
questions.explanation: Constant — used in carbon dating.

questions.question: ________ radiation is low-level radiation always present from natural sources.
questions.explanation: Radon gas is the largest natural source in the UK.

questions.question: ________ is exposure to radiation without becoming radioactive.
questions.explanation: Stops when source removed.

questions.question: ________ means radioactive material on or inside a person.
questions.explanation: More dangerous — source stays with person.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: force × perpendicular distance from pivot
clockwise = anticlockwise for balance
splits heavy nucleus — energy — power stations
joins light nuclei — powers stars
time for half of nuclei to decay — constant
